sentences.text: [CHUCKLING]
He's a spitting imagine of my father.
His nature.
His--
His--
No.
No.
He's the medical doctor.
Alan.
No.
He was-- he was trained as a philosopher.
He was-- he was teaching in [?
D ?] as a lecturer.
But now he's a business--
Camera set.
In, in 1941 it started to be really-- as-- as Germany got stronger, and you know, they came, they came into power, it influenced the Hungarian government.
Well, not, not directly.
Just because my father didn't have-- owned the store.
And he was in public service till the last day, practically the last day before they took us away.
But, but others, others who had a store who had-- we had a very good friend who was, was injured in the first World War.
He had a big gold medal.
A very fine man, he lost one leg.
And all of a sudden, took away his livelihood.
It was not respected by the government anymore, that he practically gave his life for the, for the country.
And we, we felt somehow, the remarks from the teachers, the remarks from the former neighbors, degrading.
It just, it just-- they didn't-- they didn't-- we didn't get the same treatment like everybody else.
Yes.
They were-- we started, the foolish way, it first started with the private clubs.
I had an uncle in a bigger city, and I was there once.
And they just showed me that a sign was out, no Jews and dogs allowed.
And it was hurting.
You know, it was hurting everyone.
We could live without the clubs, but the idea that why did they do this, to innocent people whom they don't know?
It, it was very painful.
And then the parliament, everything was blamed on the Jews.
And surely by that time, life was getting tough in Hungary, because the, the prime of the youth were out fighting an unnecessary war, what is not their war, really.
It just-- because they joined Hitler.
Hitler promised, actually-- this is a little bit of history here-- that Hungary, in the First World War, was an ally to Germany.
Of course, they lost the war and achieved big powers.
As a punishment, took away from Hungary the best part of the land.
The bread basket down south was given to Yugoslavia.
Even the good neighbors, Austria, took a chunk.
And Czechoslovakia came into being, took a big part.
And Romania, another part, the salt mines.
And the best, the ore and iron mines, were down in Transylvania.
It was all taken away.
And Hungary became a very poor, little nation.
And they had-- they had a chip on their shoulder.
They were very sore about it.
And we learned in school always that Hungary, this not [?
Aryan ?] country.
The whole country is a-- if we would have the whole country, it would be a heaven again.
So Hitler came and promised to these gallant Hungarians, if they join his, his army, then he would restore them to the old glory.
Foolishly, they believed him, and that's how they got into the war.
So what happens?
The Hungarians led always in the front, and Germans didn't respect them really.
And they let them go.
They were the [? forwards, ?] you know, to the gun.
They were always at front, and they-- they were out and were killed by the Russians.
And at home, they sent home the dead bodies, these young people.
So the people were angry, and they blame that on the Jews.
And the poor Jews, well, they were in-- the men were in the labor camp, and the women struggled at home to barely to keep alive with the children.
The stores were taken, every licenses.
They couldn't do hardly anything.
And they were still blamed for everything.
They needed a scapegoat.
So-- and then was it, was it a sad joke.
The minister, the prime minister, his name was Gombos.
G-O-M-B-O-S.
One day they dragged it out that his grandmother was Jewish.
So it was a sad victory for us, but he had to go.
Yeah.
Yeah, Gyula Gombos.
It was-- it was ridiculous.
But he had the biggest mouth, and he was blaming everything on the Jews.
And when they find out that-- I mean, he probably knew-- but his enemies find out that he comes from a Jewish background.
He had to resign.
Then came another one, who was even worse.
Well, we tried to-- tried to live a relatively normal life.
My mother was the one who was standing in a line for food, with the food stamps, to get some food to the table.
My father was still teaching, and I was working.
And my brothers were away studying.
It was not much disruption.
Whatever we knew where we were not welcome, we tried not to go.
And we lived in a very quiet life, very subdued.
In the beginning, yes.
In the beginning, yes.
But it was very little.
And the black market started to thrive.
And who had the money could help themselves.
But a lot of people went hungry by that time.
Everything went to Germany, especially when they marched in, in 1944.
March the 19th, I think was the date.
They marched in because our President decided that he wanted to make a private peace with, with the Allies.
And Hitler find it out.
And the same day, they marched into Hungary.
Every town, every village, was covered, was full with Nazi soldiers.
And then the real tragedy started.
Yeah, right.
I was visiting my grandparents that day, and I said goodbye to them.
And they were in a very bad-- in a low spirit, because we knew that things are not very good.
And I went home and the train station, I heard the German loud-- giving orders, loud words.
And when I got off the train, my father was waiting for me.
And I mentioned to him that in Miskolc, in a big city where I had to wait a connection, I heard of it.
I saw too many German soldiers.
The following morning, he came back from the synagogue after prayers, and was telling us that unfortunately, Germany occupied Hungary.
And that week started really.
We got out the order.
We had to-- we had to wear the yellow stars.
And Jews were restricted from a lot of things that was not allowed to do.
That was just before Passover in '44.
And within a month, we were shipped to the ghetto.
Yeah.
We had to, we had to carry that yellow star, what was a badge of, of-- supposed to be-- I felt like it's badge of honor, but we were not happy about it, certainly not.
By that time, the children were not allowed to go in to school.
Because everything was closed.
The kids were at home, and it was miserable.
And my poor mother was trying to put together the food, because there was practically-- was nothing available.
And when it was, it came not-- a lady with the Nazi ideals.
So surely, she got the first choice, and the Jews were always kicked in the back.
And well, it didn't last that long, but it was very painful.
And that was during the Passover holidays.
So we had a very sinking feeling that there's something bad is coming.
It was obvious that they didn't want-- that something is coming.
Well, it was not much, not much.
My father, as an optimist, he was a born optimist.
But by that time, he knew a lot but he didn't discuss with us.
Because the Hungarian soldiers, the gentile soldiers, came back from the front.
And they were telling stories about what had happened to these unfortunate Hungarians who didn't have their papers in order.
They were kicked over, and took over to [NON-ENGLISH].
That was a horrible place.
And in the few weeks after they shipped out, thousands of people, whole families.
Letters came back, here and there, begging for food.
But we had no way to send it to them, because we didn't-- that was war zone already.
And these Hungarian soldiers came home for a holiday, and the decent ones talked about it, that all these people were killed.
Shot.
Shot and killed.
So we never heard of them again.
So it was almost like, like a huge funeral.
Everybody knew someone who was on that train, who was shipped-- sent away.
No, no.
We knew as much that the Slovaks, from Slovakia, the young girls were gathered and sent them to work in Germany and factories.
That and some of them wrote letters back home.
And they just, it was like a-- they're always the same text.
I'm fine, I'm working.
Hope you are all right.
Love, signed.
So you know, we discussed it among ourselves, that if it's-- if they can write a letter and they are working, can it be so bad?
But we didn't-- otherwise, most of them, we didn't hear from them.
Here and there, a letter came.
What was the irony, that while we were in the ghetto already, one neighbor of ours who had four little children, husband was killed.
She knew was killed.
Red Cross let her know.
She got a letter from a cousin who lived in Czechoslovakia.
And that letter arrived to the ghetto while we were in the biggest misery.
And so she showed us this, and said now look, my cousin sent me that letter.
She's working in the factory.
So it can't be so bad.
It's a lot of people and people are talking.
Hopefully not as bad as they're talking.
So that lured other people.
Because if they got a letter and they'd say OK, they are working.
So-- and my father said that.
When the announcement came that we are going on-- we were relocated to work for the war effort in factories.
He said, as long we are together, it's all right.
Yeah.
From Szerencs, we-- they gathered us.
And within two days, all the Jews were taken out from the city, allowed a 50-kilogram bag.
No suitcases, just in a bed sheet.
Tied up whatever we could.
And walked in the middle of the road, not on the sidewalk.
First, they took us to the city hall.
There we were registered.
The police came for us.
And they--
The Hungarian police.
The corps de gendarmes, with the big feather.
They were these special-- like like here, the mounted police, they were the political.
No.
They from Budapest.
They gave the orders, But but didn't see Germans.
No.
No.
This was all done by the Hungarians.
All the dirty work was done by the Hungarians.
And came to the door with the order that this family, so many, so many persons, have to go.
And they were waiting for us, and they took us in the city hall.
Within a few hours, we were walked to the station.
What was the most painful, to see that all our good neighbors were standing, both sides of the road, looking at us or turned their head.
But nobody dared to open their mouth, to raise their voices, that what are you doing with these unfortunate people?
My father, my well-respected father, carrying that bag.
And it was the most humiliating experience.
And he was so dignified.
He said, never mind, my child.
We are together.
It hopefully will work out.
And they took us to train station, and surprisingly, the regular train.
It was not a cattle car, just a regular train.
They shoveled us in.
And we arrived in-- we had what is an hour and a half train ride.
And from there, we had to walk in to the ghetto.
As it happened, my mother had an older sister living there.
And her street was in, in the ghetto.
So we went straight to her house.
By that time, her house was loaded with people.
Beds were not allowed to sleep in.
It was too dignified.
So we just-- and there was no room for beds anyways.
So we were just-- with the blanket, what we brought with us, we just made on the floor.
And we slept there, the whole family, my two brothers, and my parents and myself.
My older brother, the same time, had called in to the army, to the labor force.
He had time to come home to say goodbye to us while we were waiting by the doorway to be taken away.
So he went on his way, he went up to-- it was Kosice, to join his battalion.
And we were taken into the ghetto.
And he never saw my parents again.
That was the last time.
And in the ghetto-- Well, they didn't give us food, and we were not allowed to go into the city.
And whatever we brought with us, it was-- didn't last too long.
And most of the people were-- find a place to lie down.
It was a brick factory.
It was just a roof over, but it was open on all sides.
And it was April, middle of April.
So it was still cold, windy and rainy.
And paper suffered.
So the Jewish community, in [NON-ENGLISH]-- first of all, they-- by that time, the Nazis were involved.
They came down and they gave out the order to the Hungarian police.
The Hungarian police picked a Jewish group of police men to do the dirty job for them.
So we were told what to do, what not.
But they closed up the ghetto with a rope, nothing more.
The one side of the street was for Gentiles, the other one was for Jews.
All the windows have to be painted white, that we shouldn't be able to look out in the street.
And it was a certain time of the day when we could cross over to the other side, where it was a part of the ghetto.
My father, as a, as a-- working for the government, for the Jewish community, he became-- there, we had a little office.
And I worked with him to make a list of names and try to gather together whatever the people brought with them.
To pull it together and made-- they made a kitchen.
How do you call it?
A Soup kitchen.
And the people were very hungry, whatever they could get.
Once a group of men were allowed, with the police taking them, back to our hometown to take out the food, what we left behind.
So they brought the food.
Nothing else they were allowed.
And this-- they made a-- they closed the door with a seal, sealed the door.
Well, we understood within a few days, the good neighbors broke the seal and they took out everything from the house.
They didn't wait too long.
So when I came home, I didn't find anything.
So in the ghetto, it was very difficult.
Very.
It was hunger, and no medication, and harassment from the police came every day.
If we have been hiding jewelry.
And atrocious accusations.
What was-- and we just didn't know.
We couldn't defend ourselves.
So when they came the order, that the first transport should be ready-- A little, just a little bit more about that.
Yes.
Yes.
Father, mother, and my two younger brothers.
The five of us.
And we stayed by my aunt's house.
There were, there were, I think, seven of the children were home.
And many others, neighbors.
Former neighbors from our town.
We all stayed in that house.
Four weeks.
Exactly four weeks.
And then, you know, still, the interesting thing, the post office was still working.
And from other town-- part of Hungary, where they were not in a ghetto, my father had an older sister.
In another part of the-- in the city-- in the country, sent us parcels.
Surprisingly, arrived.
But when how long-- how much we got-- everybody got a little bit from that it.
It wasn't enough to keep us alive.
So when they came the order that the following morning at six o'clock, we should be ready for relocation, and the order, again, that what we can carry with us, my father was relieved.
He said, at least we will be together.
Hopefully, we will go to work and we will make it.
Let's hope so.
So then my aunt said, no, no, no.
She's not going yet.
They divided the ghetto, 30,000 people, into 10,000 at a time.
It was three, three parts.
So my father said, I'm not waiting anymore.
If we are-- if it's meant to leave, we will make it.
It-- you can't live like this.
This is not human.
Let's go.
Let's go.
Let's get it over with, and hope for the best.
So we were into the first transport.
They took us into the synagogue.
Everywhere, they used the synagogue.
And we-- we didn't sleep over.
It was just for a couple of hours.
And then they line us five in a row.
They marched us out to the railway station.
What was very strange, that they brought the mental patients from the mental institution and they were a makeshift Jewish hospital.
And they took all the Jewish patients, all these patients, out.
And they brought them in wagons, and whoever, people have to carry them.
And they brought this all these sick people with us.
And that was somehow-- we started to speculate.
That why did they bring the sick people?
They can't go to work.
So anyhow, it wasn't much time to think.
We were all boarded in the, in a cattle wagon, cattle cars.
About 80, 85 people to a cattle car.
Two containers.
One for drinking water, and the other one for toilet facilities.
And somebody hung up a little-- something, had a blanket that they should have a little bit of privacy.
And were were sitting on the floor, a loaf of bread for everyone, and then the train started to roll.
When we arrived to the Czech-Hungarian border, Nazis took over from the Hungarians.
And we had a big speech in German, that if you're going to work, then we will-- we helped to win the war, then we can hope that we will survive.
And that was-- that was the-- they let us go.
That was the blessing.
And the train was rolling.
And when we-- the second day, we arrived to the Polish border, my father said-- somebody had a map with him.
And they looked at it, and said, we are not going to Germany.
They're taking us to Poland.
That's not good.
And-- but we couldn't do anything.
We were closed in like animals in a-- there was a little window in the top, chicken wire.
We couldn't-- and the kids were dying from thirst.
Crying, and fighting, and miserable.
So it was two days and a night, we traveled.
And it was a bright, sunny day.
Because we heard that in Poland, a lot of others-- a lot of bad things happened.
We didn't know exactly what.
But we knew that Poland is a killing field.
We knew that people who were out-- went-- were taken out, they never returned.
And that was something to worry about.
We didn't worry about going to Germany, because we figured we will work.
But Poland was something else.
And my father probably heard what's going on in camps. not in details.
But in camps, here and there, somebody escaped.
And tried to-- one was Mr.
Mendel something-- I can't remember his name-- from Czechoslovakia.
He went all the way to [?
Zurich. ?] And tried to raise the consciousness of the Jews, that's what's happening.
And sent letters to America and everywhere.
But nobody, nobody took him seriously.
It was just too horrible.
I think so.
I find out after when we arrived, from his last word to me.
Then I realized that he knew more than he was telling us, what he let us know.
So it was a bright, sunny day, and around 10 o'clock in the morning.
There were still-- were prayers.
They made the Shabbat prayers.
In the wagon, we had a rabbi with us from a nearby town.
And all of the sudden, the doors were open.
And we saw these funny looking guys with the striped uniform.
Men.
They spoke Yiddish.
And the Nazis and them and [GERMAN] quick!
Get out!
[GERMAN] And when the Nazi's didn't look, didn't hear, they were telling the young mothers, give your child, give your child to your mother.
Give it to the grandmother.
Let her carry the child.
And didn't understand what it is.
So of course, most of the young mothers didn't want to part with their children.
So they were standing first-- as we came down, five in a row.
And then a gentleman, if you call him a gentleman.
Dr.
Mengele, who I find out later on, he was standing there with the black boots, with the black gloves in his hand.
The stick in his hand, and started to point.
Left and right, and left and right.
And before that, when we got-- just about before we left the train, my father blessed me.
And he said, I know that we are going to be separated.
And they're going to send you to work.
I ask two things from you.
Whatever they give you to eat, you eat because you need the energy to survive.
We were Kosher, so I never ate anything else.
He said, never mind.
Whatever they give you, you eat.
And when they call to work, you should be the first one to go.
And that was the last.
Before I had a chance to kiss him, or say goodbye, or anything, he disappeared with my brother, the 16 years old.
I was still--
I was still standing with my mother and holding my little brother, Emil, in the middle.
On the-- down from the--
Yeah.
Come down from the train.
Nothing-- we couldn't take anything out but the possession that we brought with us.
But it was not important.
We were so scared by that time.
So in front of us was standing four girls, who were sleeping in the same room with us, with their father and three little grandchildren.
Their parents were hiding in Budapest.
They were from Czechoslovakia.
To [? say ?] to send these three kids down to grandfather, maybe safer in Hungary.
So when they were picked up, we were together in the ghetto.
So of course, they brought these little children with them.
It was-- between three to nine or something.
I can't even remember if they were boys or girls.
And their grandfather.
So they snatched the grandfather away.
The four sisters were holding into the three little kids.
When my mother saw that Mengele is making rows of five, he-- she pulled the three kids to her and pushed me to be the fifth with these four girls, the tall, good looking girls.
And she meant-- she told them, I'm going to take care of the children, and you take care of Judy.
How did she know?
How did she feel that this is the right thing to do?
And I just turned to her, tell her that no, mother, I want to go with you.
She said, no.
You just go with them.
And a minute later, she disappeared.
I never saw her again.
So she took the four little children with her to the gas chamber, and that's how I survived.
These four girls were really good to me.
Until they came-- and the first selection, and we got the number.
And nobody knew that we are not sisters.
They were my height, and we were very close.
They were really good to me.
And then the next thing I find myself in the bath house.
Well, my brother went with my father, with the men.
The youngest-- well, I had two brothers, and my older brother was in the army already.
But the little one went with my mother.
And the 16-year-old, Simon, went with my father.
And that was it.
It just went so quick that I turned my head, I didn't see them anymore.
It was thousands of people came in.
And the music was playing in the corner.
It was right by the front of the gate, with the fancy sign, that Arbeit Macht Frei.
That infamous saying.
Nobody got free to work in Birkenau Auschwitz.
And the women orchestra was playing in the corner, in a platform, the most beautiful music.
So we were numb.
We didn't appreciate the music so much, but it gave a little bit of comfort, that if it's music playing, it can't be so bad.
Killing didn't enter my mind, that there is such a thing, that people, healthy people, should be killed.
So it was not much time to think, that within minutes, we were herded in the right and into a bathhouse.
It was-- that was some bath house, with the hanging the water-- you know, the shower heads.
And they called-- the SS called, is there any barber among you?
